mnemonics: Singular transforms 'squish' space (determinant 0), Non-singular transforms 'preserve' space (determinant ≠ 0). Think: SINGular = SQUISHed into lower dimension.
keyPoints: A linear transformation T: V → W is singular if it is not one-to-one (injective).
A transformation is non-singular if it is both one-to-one and onto (bijective) for finite-dimensional spaces, equivalent to having an inverse.
Singularity is determined by the determinant of the transformation's matrix representation: det(A) = 0 for singular, det(A) ≠ 0 for non-singular.
commonMistakes: Assuming a non-singular transformation must be onto (surjective). It only guarantees one-to-one for same-dimensional spaces.
Confusing the terms 'singular' and 'non-invertible'. They are equivalent for square matrices but the concepts apply more generally to linear maps between spaces.
